summary: Gruber offers detailed final predictions for Apple Watch pricing ahead of the March 2015 event, arguing that the steel Apple Watch collection will be far more expensive than most expect, with bands serving as major price differentiators rather than interchangeable accessories. He predicts a continuum from $349 to potentially $20,000, with the steel Link Bracelet model reaching $1,500-$2,000. He argues Apple's September messaging created misleading price expectations by emphasizing personal style over cost stratification. He also dismisses the S1 upgradeability theory, predicts third-party bands will flourish, and anticipates the event will include the new MacBook Air and Photos app alongside extensive Watch coverage.
key_points: Watch bands will function as stratifying price differentiators, not interchangeable accessories, with the Link Bracelet alone worth roughly $1,000 based on manufacturing complexity and luxury watch comparables

The steel Apple Watch must start at roughly double the Sport price ($749+) because the extensive material differences between collections wouldn't justify Apple's operational complexity for a mere $150 upsell
Apple's September messaging created false expectations by emphasizing style choice over price tiers, and the steel collection's pricing will trigger the biggest backlash
The S1 chip upgradeability theory doesn't hold up because future improvements will span display, sensors, battery, and networking — not just the processor
At $349, Apple Watch has the lowest entry-level price of any first-generation Apple flagship product
money_quotes: Apple tends to be such a predictable company that we often know the basic gist of what to expect before one of their media events. Not this time.
Apple sets prices not based on what people want to pay, but what people are willing to pay.
This is without question new territory for Apple. They've never sold products with the same computing internals at different pricing tiers based solely on the luxuriousness of the materials.
Sport and steel only make sense as separate collections if the steel collection is significantly higher in price, even at the entry level with the rubber Sport band.
If the marketing argument doesn't persuade you, the operations angle should.
The 'computer' inside Apple Watch isn't centrally important. Everything is important.
Trading in used goods is not part of a luxury shopping experience.
I'm out on a limb here, and it's quite possible I'll be serving up some home-cooked claim chowder Monday.
key_insight: Apple Watch bands are not interchangeable style accessories but stratifying price differentiators that create a pricing continuum from $349 to $20,000, making the steel collection's cost — not the gold Edition's — the real surprise.
